items.question: Who needs to care about random seeds in ML experiments?
items.answer: Data scientists, ML engineers, and researchers who compare models, publish results, or debug flaky training — anyone whose conclusions depend on whether a metric change is real or shuffle luck.

items.question: What is a random seed in this context?
items.answer: An integer (or vector) that initializes a pseudo-random number generator so the same sequence of random draws — shuffles, initializations, augmentations — repeats when the same seed and software stack are used.

items.question: When should you set seeds in a training pipeline?
items.answer: Once at program start, before any random operation: data shuffling, weight initialization, dropout, mini-batch order, and CV fold assignment. Also log the seed value when the run starts.

items.question: Where do seeds get applied in a typical Python ML stack?
items.answer: Python random, NumPy, PyTorch or TensorFlow, sklearn random_state on splits and CV, DataLoader generator for batch shuffle, and optional CUDA deterministic flags on GPU — each is a separate RNG stream.

items.question: Why is reproducibility more than just picking a seed?
items.answer: Comparable reruns also need the same code commit, data version, hyperparameters, and pinned environment (Python and library versions, CUDA). Seeds control intentional randomness; pinning controls runtime behavior.

items.question: How do you verify an experiment is reproducible?
items.answer: Checkout the logged git commit, restore the pinned environment, apply the logged seed(s), rerun training, and compare metrics within an acceptable tolerance — investigating GPU nondeterminism or data drift if they diverge.

items.question: Random seed vs environment pinning — what does each fix?
items.answer: A seed fixes the sequence of pseudo-random choices inside a run. Environment pinning fixes which library and hardware behavior executes the code. You need both for teammates to approximate your metrics on another machine.

items.question: Single-seed run vs multi-seed benchmark — when to use which?
items.answer: Single fixed seed is ideal for development sweeps where you hold everything constant except one hyperparameter. Multi-seed runs (mean ± std) are better for final claims and papers when stochastic training variance is non-trivial.

items.question: CPU deterministic training vs default GPU training?
items.answer: CPU runs with fixed seeds often match closely across reruns. Default GPU training may differ due to nondeterministic reductions unless strict deterministic flags are enabled — usually slower but closer to bitwise repeatability.

items.question: What if you forget to seed the DataLoader shuffle?
items.answer: Mini-batch order changes every epoch even with fixed weight-init seed — training curves and final metrics drift run to run, making hyperparameter comparisons unreliable until you pass a seeded generator or disable shuffle.

items.question: What if you upgrade PyTorch but keep the same seed?
items.answer: Operator implementations may change; you might see small metric shifts despite identical seed and data. Re-pin the library version or re-benchmark after upgrades — Versioning Experiments tracks artifact lineage for this case.

items.question: Cause: Log seed=42 and git commit but omit requirements.txt. Effect?
items.answer: A teammate reruns with newer sklearn or PyTorch and gets different splits or numerics — apparent reproducibility failure that is actually environment drift, not a bad seed.

items.question: Cause: Enable torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True. Effect?
items.answer: Many GPU ops become repeatable across reruns with the same seed, but training may slow down and some fast nondeterministic kernels are disabled — trading speed for stricter repeatability.
